title: Breaking bad news: acknowledge emotion (SPIKES)
vignette: A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic to discuss the results of a recent abdominal CT scan. The patient says, "I hope the scan can tell me why I've been losing so much weight. What does it show?" The physician shares that the imaging is suggestive of pancreatic cancer. Upon hearing this, the patient begins to cry while holding her head in her hands. Which of the following is the most appropriate response by the physician?

clues.text: discuss the results of a recent abdominal CT scan
clues.note: A results visit where serious findings are expected is a 'breaking bad news' scenario; the SPIKES framework structures how to deliver it (Setting, Perception, Invitation, Knowledge, Empathy, Strategy).
Unintentional weight loss is the presenting concern (a red-flag pancreatic cancer symptom). It sets up the gravity of the news to follow.

clues.text: imaging is suggestive of pancreatic cancer
clues.note: Serious, life-altering news has just been delivered. The very next physician action should respond to EMOTION, not pivot to logistics, prognosis, or data.

clues.text: begins to cry while holding her head in her hands
clues.note: An acute emotional reaction is a cue to use an EMPATHIC statement: name/acknowledge the emotion, express support, and give the patient time before continuing. This is the 'E' (Empathy) step of SPIKES.

whatMatters: After delivering serious news to a crying patient, the most appropriate response ACKNOWLEDGES her distress, expresses empathy, and gives her time to process - without redirecting to data, prognosis, or logistics. Answer B ('I wish I had better news... I imagine this is very upsetting to hear'). Let the patient lead what comes next.
strategy: In bad-news communication, EMOTION comes before information. When a patient is crying, pick the option that names/validates the feeling and pauses - not the one that explains results (too soon), redirects to coping resources (premature), offers false optimism (dismissive), or defers the conversation (avoidant). Empathic acknowledgment first; patient sets the pace.

options.text: "I know this isn't the news you wanted to hear. Let's talk about the results in a bit more detail."
"I wish I had better news to share with you. I imagine this is very upsetting to hear."
"It's easy to feel lonely and isolated with news like this. Tell me about the people who support you during difficult times."
"This must feel very shocking and unexpected. Fortunately, there have been some advances in treatment recently."
"This news would be upsetting to anyone. Let's set up an appointment to discuss the next steps in a few days."

eliminations.label: Redirect to the results - too soon
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a brief acknowledgment followed by an immediate pivot to discussing results in detail. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it redirects to information before the patient has processed her emotions; she should be allowed to cry and lead. ✅ Correct if: the patient has composed herself and asks to go through the results.

eliminations.label: Pivot to support system - premature
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: shifts the conversation to who supports her at home. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it prematurely changes the subject away from her immediate emotional reaction; address the present feeling first. ✅ Correct if: later, when exploring coping and psychosocial support.

eliminations.label: False optimism / reassurance - dismissive
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: acknowledges shock but quickly reassures with 'advances in treatment.' ❌ WHY IT FAILS: premature optimism can shut down her expression of fear and emotion; empathy should come before reassurance. ✅ Correct if: after emotions are acknowledged and she asks about treatment/prognosis.

eliminations.label: Defer the conversation - avoidant
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: validates that anyone would be upset, then proposes meeting in a few days. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it assumes she doesn't want to engage now and avoids supporting her in the moment; stay present and let her decide the pace. ✅ Correct if: the patient herself asks to pause and return another day.

reasoning: Serious news (pancreatic cancer) was just delivered and the patient is crying
The next step is to respond to EMOTION, not information (SPIKES 'Empathy' step)
The best response names/acknowledges her distress, expresses empathy, and gives her time
-> empathic acknowledgment without redirection; answer B
mechanism: Frameworks such as SPIKES (Setting, Perception, Invitation, Knowledge, Empathy, Strategy/Summary) guide delivery of serious news. When a patient reacts emotionally, the physician should use an empathic response - acknowledging and naming the emotion, expressing support, and allowing silence/time - before providing further information. Redirecting to results, prognosis, treatment optimism, support systems, or scheduling all move past the patient's immediate emotional need and can suppress her expression of feelings. After the patient regains composure, the physician follows her lead, gently exploring how much she wishes to know and answering her questions.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Behavioral science > delivering serious news: SPIKES protocol; respond to emotion with empathic statements; allow the patient to process and lead.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: The stem asks for the NEXT response to a crying patient. The task is to pick the empathic, emotion-first option, not the most informative one.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on SPIKES, empathic statement, 'follow the patient's lead,' or false reassurance? Tell me which.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Every option sounds compassionate, which is the trap. A (results), C (support system), D (optimism), and E (defer) all move PAST the emotion; only B stays with it.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank: bad news + crying patient = acknowledge/validate the emotion and pause (empathy before information); avoid redirecting, false reassurance, or deferring.

keywords.front: Patient cries after serious news - best response
keywords.back: Acknowledge/name the emotion, express empathy, allow time (don't redirect)
Setting, Perception, Invitation, Knowledge, Empathy, Strategy/Summary
Address emotion FIRST, then provide information at the patient's pace
Premature reassurance suppresses the patient's expression of fear/emotion
Follow the patient's lead; ask how much she wants to know

firstAid.detail: Behavioral science > empathic acknowledgment of emotion before information; allow processing time; patient leads.
firstAid.location: Behavioral science > empathic acknowledgment of emotion before information; allow processing time; patient leads.

firstAid.detail: Behavioral science > avoid premature redirection, false reassurance, deferring, or changing the subject.
firstAid.location: Behavioral science > avoid premature redirection, false reassurance, deferring, or changing the subject.

firstAid.detail: Behavioral science > open-ended questions, silence, reflective/empathic statements.
firstAid.location: Behavioral science > open-ended questions, silence, reflective/empathic statements.

memoryHook: When the tears come, sit in the moment. Name the feeling first ('I imagine this is very upsetting'); the facts can wait until she's ready.
